title: Cases - MylwardV.Weldon
content:
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻[The plaintiff was committed to the Fleet Prison on Feb. 8, 1596, by order of the Lord Keeper, for drawing a replication of sixscore sheets containing much impertinent matter which might well have been contained in sixteen. On Feb. 10 the Lord Keeper ordered that on the following Saturday the Warden of the Fleet should cut a hole through the replication, and put the plaintiff's head through the hole and let it hang about his shoulders with the written side outwards, and lead the plaintiff bareheaded and barefaced round about Westminster Hall, and show him at the bar of all the courts, and so back to the Fleet.--Abridged from Spence's Equitable Jurisdiction, vol. i. p. 376.]

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Gainst Weldon Mylward files a bill,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻But doth his replication fill
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻With scandalous and idle matter,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻That would disgrace the maddest hatter.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Woe is me for Mylward!

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻'Twas sixscore sheets, it might have been
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Contained, and amply, in sixteen;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻So after that the court hath risen
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Must Mylward Fleetward go to prison.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Woe is me for Mylward!

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻And two days afterwards 'tis meet
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻That by the Warden of the Fleet
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻He be led on in slow progression
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Through every court that sits in session.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Woe is me for Mylward!

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The pleading writ with words so fair
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Must Mylward like a tabard wear,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻A hole therein, the Warden cuts it,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻A head put through it, Mylward puts it.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Woe is me for Mylward!

혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻The bar makes merry at his shame;
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻What careth he? He winneth fame,
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Three hundred years his reputation
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Hath rested on that replication.
혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻혻Woe is me for Mylward!
meaning:
James Williams's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "Cases - MylwardV.Weldon"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
